file_name: Non Disclosure Agreement - CP Specific NDA (CreativeNDA-CPS121.1014).txt

perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Inf ormation:” means all confidential information (however recorded, preserved or disclosed) disclosed by a Party or its Representatives to the other Party and that Party's Representa tives including but not limited to: the fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; any inform ation that would be regarded as confidential by a reasonable business person relating to: (a) the business, affai rs, customers, clients, suppliers, plans , inte ntions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; (b) the operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, specificatio ns, trade secrets or so ftware of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Group; and (c) any information or analysis derived from Confidential Inform ation; but not including any info rmation: 1 Creative NDA-CPS121.1014 / DLR: 01-15-18 (a) that is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of its disclosure by the Recipient or its Representatives in breach of this Agreement or of any other undertaking of confidentiality addressed to the Party to whom the information relates (except that any compilation of otherwise public information in a form not publicly known shall nevertheless betreated as Confidential Information); (b) was available to the Recipient on a non- confidential basis prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party; (c) was, is or becomes available to the Recipient on a non-confidential basis from a person who, to the Recipient's knowledge, is not bound by a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Partyor otherwise prohibited from disclosing the information to the Recipient; (d) was lawfully in the possession of the Recipient before the information was disclosed to it by the Disclosing Party; (e) the Parties agree in writing is not confidential or may be disclosed; and/or (f) is developed by or for the Recipient independently of the information disclosed by theDisclosing Pa rty.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Inf ormation:” means all information disclosed by a Party or its Representatives to the other Party and that Party's Representa tives relating to the Purpose and the status of discussions and negotiations. Information publicly available before the agreement is excluded. Details regarding trade secrets, customer lists, and financial models are specifically not included in the definition of confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exhaustive list of what constitutes 'Confidential Information' in the original text, and explicitly excluding trade secrets, customer lists, and financial models, we are creating an in-text contradiction. Section 4.1 states 'All Conf idential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each Party reserves all rights in its Confidential Information'. However, the exclusion in the definition of Confidential Information now places these elements outside of its protection, creating contradiction on what the disclosing party has rights over.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Conf idential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each Party reserves all rights in its Confidential Information. No rights, including, but not limited to, intellectual property rights, in respect of a party's Confidential Inform ation are granted to the other P arty and no obligations are imposed on the Disclosing Party other than those expressly stated in this A greement. Any background intellectual property r ights subsisting in a ny information provided by either Party to the other shall remain vested in the Disclosing Party and the Disclosing Party shall grant to the Recipient a non- exclusive, worldwide, royalty free, revocable licence to use such background Intellectual 4 Creative NDA-CPS121.1014 / DLR: 01-15-18 Property Rights solely for the period of this Agreement and for the Purpose only. Any foreground intellec tual property r ights generated by CP in respect of the Purpose shall vest in CP absolutely and the Company shall acquire no interest , right or title in respect of the same.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' by explicitly excluding trade secrets, customer lists, and financial models. This directly contradicts the statement in the 'Contradicted Text' that 'All Conf idential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each Party reserves all rights in its Confidential Information.' Because trade secrets, customer lists and financial models are no longer considered Confidential Information per the Changed Text, the Disclosing Party no longer retains all rights to them, creating a strong and direct contradiction. This adheres to the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.1 The Recipi ent shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Part y, shall: (a) not use or ex ploit the Confidential Information in any way except for the Purpose; (b) not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party, except as expressly permitted by this A greement; (c) not copy, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Information except as strictlynecessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be theproperty of the Disclosing Party); (d) keep separate the Confidential Information from all documents and other records of theRecipient; (e) apply the same security measures and degree of care to the Confidential Information as theRecipient applies to its own confidential information, which the Recipient warrants as providingadequate p rotection from unauthorised disclosure, copying or use; and (f) keep a written record of: any document or other Confidential Information received from the otherin tangible form; any copy made of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1 The Recipi ent shall keep the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Disclosing Part y, shall: (a) use the Confidential Information only with prior written consent; (b) disclose the Confidential Information only if legally obligated; (c) apply reasonable security measures to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause outlines specific obligations on how the Recipient should handle Confidential Information. By severely shortening this and removing explicit prohibitions (like not exploiting the information, copying it, or keeping it separate), we are creating an in-text contradiction. Section 5.2 mentions about indemnity if the Recipient breaches the agreement. With the change, it's now unclear what constitutes a breach related to Confidential Information handling, as the specific obligations are significantly weakened, reducing its ability to be enforced.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Recipi ent shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party and its Group at all times against all liabilities, costs (includ ing legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party and/or its Group) a rising from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The original text outlines specific obligations for handling confidential information. The changed text significantly weakens these obligations by removing explicit prohibitions, making it unclear what constitutes a breach. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that the Recipient shall indemnify the Disclosing Party against any breach of the Agreement. With the weakened obligations, it becomes difficult to determine what actions would trigger the indemnity, creating a strong in-text contradiction as per the Omissions - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 2.5 The Recipi ent shall establish and maintain adequate security measures (including any reasonable security measures proposed by the Disclosing party from time to time) to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorised access or use.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 The Recipi ent should consider implementing security measures to protect Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a clear obligation on the Recipient to 'establish and maintain adequate security measures'. The changed text weakens this to a suggestion to 'consider implementing' security measures. This creates a contradiction, particularly when read in conjunction with Section 5.2, which states that the Recipient shall indemnify the Disclosing Party against losses arising from any breach of the agreement. Because 'adequate security measures' is not defined, and the obligation to implement them has been weakened, it's unclear if failure to do so will be considered a breach of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Recipi ent shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party and its Group at all times against all liabilities, costs (includ ing legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party and/or its Group) a rising from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the Recipient to 'establish and maintain adequate security measures'. The changed text weakens this obligation to merely 'consider implementing' security measures. This creates a direct contradiction with the contradicted text, which states that the Recipient shall indemnify the Disclosing Party against losses arising from any breach of the agreement. The original text created a clear and enforceable obligation. The changed text introduces ambiguity, making it unclear whether a failure to implement security measures, and subsequent breach, would trigger the indemnity clause. The change weakens the obligation, thus creating an omission that impacts the other text.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 At the r equest of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) save in r elation to its backup email systems, erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information from its computer systems or whi ch is stored in electronic form; and (c) certi fy in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this Clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflecting, incorporating, orbased on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or anyapplicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipientto keep evidence that it has performed its obligations under this A greement. The provisions of this Agreement shall continue to apply to any documents and materials retained by the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 At the r equest of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall return readily available documents containing the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement to 'destroy' information and limiting the return to only 'readily available documents', and by removing the need for certification, we create a contradiction in conjunction with section 4.1, where it states 'All Conf idential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party'. The disclosing party is the owner of the confidential information, but the modified agreement doesn't have a good way to take it back with them once the contract is terminated, creating uncertainty to the extend of ownership of the 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Conf idential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. Each Party reserves all rights in its Confidential Information. No rights, including, but not limited to, intellectual property rights, in respect of a party's Confidential Inform ation are granted to the other P arty and no obligations are imposed on the Disclosing Party other than those expressly stated in this A greement. Any background intellectual property r ights subsisting in a ny information provided by either Party to the other shall remain vested in the Disclosing Party and the Disclosing Party shall grant to the Recipient a non- exclusive, worldwide, royalty free, revocable licence to use such background Intellectual 4 Creative NDA-CPS121.1014 / DLR: 01-15-18 Property Rights solely for the period of this Agreement and for the Purpose only. Any foreground intellec tual property r ights generated by CP in respect of the Purpose shall vest in CP absolutely and the Company shall acquire no interest , right or title in respect of the same.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the recipient to 'destroy or return' confidential information, erase it from systems, and certify compliance. The changed text significantly weakens this by only requiring the return of 'readily available documents' and removing the erasure and certification requirements. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states, 'All Conf idential Information shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party.' The weakened return obligation in the changed text creates a contradiction because the disclosing party is no longer ensured a robust mechanism to reclaim its confidential information, creating a strong uncertainty about the ownership, and violating the intent of the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: 4.4 The Recipi ent acknowledges that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for the breach of any of the provisions of this Agreement. Accordingly, without prejudice to any other rights and remedies it may have, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to the granting of equitable relief (including without limitation injunctive relief) concerning any threatened or actual breach of any of the provisions of this A greement.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.4 The Recipi ent acknowledges that damages are the primary remedy for any breach of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly states that damages alone are *not* an adequate remedy, and that the Disclosing Party is entitled to equitable relief (like an injunction). The modified text directly contradicts this by stating that damages *are* the primary remedy. This creates an immediate in-text contradiction, as Section 5 continues to state the original stance in 4.4, creating confusion.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Recipi ent acknowledges that damages are the primary remedy for any breach of the provisions of this Agreement. Accordingly, without prejudice to any other rights and remedies it may have, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to the granting of equitable relief (including without limitation injunctive relief) concerning any threatened or actual breach of any of the provisions of this A greement.
perturbation.justification: The original text states damages alone are insufficient and allows for equitable relief. The changed text directly contradicts this by asserting damages are the primary remedy. The contradicted text repeats the changed text. This creates a clear in-text contradiction according to the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition, as the removal of the availability of equitable relief conflicts directly with the implication in the contradicted text, which contains the changed text, that equitable relief is an option if damages aren't adequate.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Governing Law and Jurisdiction This A greement and any di spute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of Delaware, of the United States of America, without regard to conflict of law rules thereof. The P arties irrevocably agree that the C ourts o f Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non- contractual disputes or claims). This A greement has bee n entered into on the date stated at the beginning of it.
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Governing Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the state where the Recipient's principal place of business is located. The parties agree to attempt mediation before pursuing legal action.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies Delaware law and exclusive jurisdiction of Delaware courts. The changed text replaces this with the law of the Recipient's principal place of business and mandates mediation. This directly contradicts the explicit agreement on Delaware law and jurisdiction. This also contradicts Section 5.2 as mediation is now required.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Recipi ent shall indemnify and keep fully indemnified the Disclosing Party and its Group at all times against all liabilities, costs (includ ing legal costs on an indemnity basis), expenses, damages and losses (including any direct, indirect or consequential losses, loss of profit, loss of reputation and all interest, penalties and other reasonable costs and expenses suffered or incurred by the Disclosing Party and/or its Group) a rising from any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and from the actions or omissions of any Representative of the Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies Delaware law and exclusive jurisdiction of Delaware courts. The changed text replaces this with the law of the Recipient's principal place of business and mandates mediation. This directly contradicts the explicit agreement on Delaware law and jurisdiction outlined in the original text of Section 16. Furthermore, the requirement for mediation introduced in the changed text now contradicts Section 5.2, which outlines indemnification for breaches of the agreement without mentioning mediation. This omission of mediation in the original agreement and its introduction in the changed text creates a clear contradiction regarding the process for resolving disputes arising from breaches.
